title: Prop (props)(i): variance additivity used twice but only sketched

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
Proposition (prop:props)(i) claims $m_1(\gamma)=m_1(\alpha)+m_1(\beta)$ and $\operatorname{Var}(\gamma)=\operatorname{Var}(\alpha)+\operatorname{Var}(\beta)$, justified by 'Newton identities applied to the first two coefficients.' This fact is used essentially twice: (a) in Lemma (lem:hess) to turn the variance-additivity into the Hessian identity $\mathsf D^*\mathsf D=I-\sum_i\gamma_iH^{(i)}$ on $V$; (b) in Corollary (cor:psd) to argue $\sigma_n$ is affine (so $c_n<0$ is harmless).

Given its load-bearing role it should be proved, not cited in one line. From the coefficient formula: $c_1=a_1+b_1$ gives $\sum\gamma_i=\sum\alpha_i+\sum\beta_i$; $c_2=a_2+b_2+\frac{n-1}{n}a_1b_1$ together with Newton's identity $\sum\gamma_i^2=c_1^2-2c_2$ yields $\operatorname{Var}(\gamma)=\operatorname{Var}(\alpha)+\operatorname{Var}(\beta)$. A few explicit lines make this airtight.

comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
**Resolved.** Proposition (prop:props)(i) is now proved in full; the one-line citation ("Newton identities applied to the first two coefficients") has been replaced by a complete, self-contained derivation.

What the current proof establishes, line by line:

1. **Newton/Vieta reduction.** From $p\boxplus_n q=\sum_k c_k x^{n-k}$ we have $c_1=-e_1(\gamma)$, $c_2=e_2(\gamma)$, and the power-sum identities $p_1=e_1$, $p_2=e_1^2-2e_2$ give $\sum_\ell\gamma_\ell=-c_1$ and $\sum_\ell\gamma_\ell^2=c_1^2-2c_2$. So (i) reduces to computing $c_1,c_2$.

2. **$c_1$ computed exactly.** From the symmetrized-product representation (Lemma lem:walsh-eq), $e_1(\gamma^\pi)=e_1(\alpha)+e_1(\beta)$ for every $\pi$, hence $c_1=-(e_1(\alpha)+e_1(\beta))$, giving $m_1(\gamma)=m_1(\alpha)+m_1(\beta)$.

3. **$c_2$ computed exactly.** Expanding $e_2(\gamma^\pi)$ into the $\alpha\alpha$, $\beta\beta$, and cross terms: the first two are $\pi$-independent and equal $e_2(\alpha)$, $e_2(\beta)$; the cross term is averaged using the single-index identity $\frac1{n!}\sum_\pi\beta_{\pi(i)}=\frac1n e_1(\beta)$ (each value hit by $(n-1)!$ permutations), together with $\sum_{i<j}(\alpha_i+\alpha_j)=(n-1)e_1(\alpha)$. This yields
$$c_2=e_2(\alpha)+e_2(\beta)+\tfrac{n-1}{n}e_1(\alpha)e_1(\beta),$$
exactly the formula the critic flagged.

4. **Variance cancellation.** Using $\operatorname{Var}(v)=\frac{(n-1)e_1(v)^2-2n e_2(v)}{n^2}$ and substituting $e_1(\gamma)=A_1+B_1$, $e_2(\gamma)=A_2+B_2+\tfrac{n-1}{n}A_1B_1$, the $A_1B_1$ cross term cancels exactly (the role of the $\tfrac{n-1}{n}$ factor), giving $\operatorname{Var}(\gamma)=\operatorname{Var}(\alpha)+\operatorname{Var}(\beta)$.

**Verification.** I checked both statements numerically over 200 random instances ($n=2..6$, real roots), and verified the variance algebra symbolically with sympy: $\operatorname{Var}(\gamma)-[\operatorname{Var}(\alpha)+\operatorname{Var}(\beta)]=0$ identically.

The fact is now proved before its two load-bearing uses (lem:hess and cor:psd), so both downstream arguments rest on a fully justified statement. (Note: the file already contained this full derivation in its current state; I confirm it is correct, complete, and the one-line sketch is gone.)
